mnemonics.title: BRD - Base Recurse Divide
mnemonics.content: Remember the three key components of recursive functions: B (Base case - stops recursion), R (Recursive case - calls itself), D (Divide - breaks problem into smaller parts). For linear binary search: Linear searches one by one, Binary divides in half each time.

mnemonics.title: T-Rex Can't Properly Find Dinner
mnemonics.content: T-Rex = T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) for Binary Search. Can't = Constant time per level. Properly = Proportional to log n. Find = Time complexity. Dinner = Divide and conquer.

mnemonics.title: Little Elephants Are Small In Size
mnemonics.content: LEASS = Linear search: O(n) - Linear Time. For Binary search remember: O(log n) - Logarithmic Time. Elephants = Exponential (2^n), Are = Arithmetic (n^k), Small = Linear (n), In = Logarithmic (log n), Size = Constant (1). Order from slowest to fastest.

acronyms.usage: Every recursive function must have a BCR - Base case to stop recursion and Recursive case to call itself with smaller input.
Binary search uses D&C - divide the sorted array in half, conquer (search) the appropriate half, combine (return result).
Describes how recursive call stack works - last function called is the first to complete, like a stack of plates.

acronyms.expansion: Time complexity of problem size n
acronyms.usage: Used to express running time. For linear recursive search: T(n) = T(n-1) + O(1). For binary: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1).

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a stack of plates being built up (function calls) and then unwound (returns). Each plate is a function frame with parameters. In binary search, the stack gets deeper as you keep dividing, then unwinds as you return results.

Picture a balanced tree where each node splits into two children representing the mid-point divisions. The root is the first mid, left child is lower half, right child is upper half. Search is like climbing down this tree following comparisons.
Think of a conveyor belt with items. Each recursive call checks one item and moves to the next. Visualize a pointer sliding one position at a time until target is found or end is reached.

Visualize two pointers (low and high) like bookends shrinking toward each other. The middle (mid) pointer always jumps to the exact center of the current interval, cutting search space in half each time.

analogies.analogy: Looking for a specific book in an unsorted pile by checking each book one by one from top to bottom. You check one, if not found, move to next - exactly like checking each element sequentially.

Looking up a word in a dictionary. You don't start from page 1; you open to the middle, see if your word is before or after, then repeat only in that half. This is why binary search requires sorted data.
The exit door in a maze. Without it, you could keep walking endlessly. Base case is the condition that tells you 'found it' or 'not here' and lets you stop recursing.

Asking someone to solve a smaller version of the same problem. Like asking 'What's 100+99+98...?' you might say '99+98 first' - breaking it into smaller identical problems until you reach a simple answer you know.
